title: Core value case
text: Pathogen genomics is useful when higher-resolution evidence changes public-health action: detecting linked cases, ruling clusters in or out, monitoring pathogen evolution, supporting antimicrobial-resistance surveillance, and helping teams decide where to investigate or intervene.
reviewerNotes: Reviewed after WHO 2022, Armstrong 2019, and PHE evidence items were backed by source-text locators.

title: Economic caution
text: The economic case should be made with setting-specific evidence. Some services may save time, reduce duplicated testing, shorten reporting, or focus outbreak investigation more efficiently, but others face higher near-term costs for infrastructure, validation, training, quality systems, data management, and support.
reviewerNotes: Reviewed after Price et al. economic-review evidence was converted from source-card summary to passage-level evidence.

title: Distributed capability and equity
text: For surveillance, value depends on where capability exists and whether data flow regularly. Distributed sequencing and reporting capacity can improve early detection and preparedness compared with expanding capacity only in already high-capacity settings, but the current beta guide should not turn respiratory-virus modelling thresholds into universal targets for all pathogens.
reviewerNotes: Do not include the model's numeric capacity threshold until model assumptions and transferability limits are extracted.

title: Value-case evidence to collect
text: A credible value case should collect evidence about the public-health decision that will change, the operational improvement compared with current practice, the recurring costs and responsibilities created by the service, and whether published economic evidence is transferable to the planned setting. The table should prevent generic return-on-investment claims where the source base is heterogeneous or setting-specific.
reviewerNotes: This is a beta value-case evidence table. It does not support universal ROI or cost-effectiveness claims.

title: Choose the economic strategy before costing
text: Before making a cost or investment argument, distinguish pilot sequencing, outbreak-triggered sequencing, selective routine surveillance, continuous surveillance and national investment-case planning. These strategies create different recurring costs, detection benefits, response dependencies, workforce needs and evidence requirements.
reviewerNotes: This is a beta strategy-choice table. It does not support universal ROI, cost-per-sample or sampling-fraction claims.

title: Operating model core
text: A genomics infrastructure choice is also a responsibility choice. The service must decide who operates each layer, who validates workflows, who supports users, who manages access and storage, and who carries the risk when service delivery fails.
reviewerNotes: Likely belongs in the public guide after review.

title: If bioinformatics staff are not available
text: If there is no stable bioinformatics staffing, the implementation plan should not depend on one person maintaining scripts, environments, reference data, and troubleshooting. Consider managed platforms, shared national or regional services, external support agreements, or a narrower validated workflow while local capability is built.
reviewerNotes: Needs review for strength of recommendation and source coverage. Do not approve until the bioinformatics implementation claim is source-text backed.

title: Sample-to-report lifecycle
text: A sequencing programme receives samples or isolates, links them to metadata, generates sequence data, checks quality, runs analysis, interprets outputs, produces reports, shares selected data, stores records, and decides what should be archived or deleted.
reviewerNotes: Candidate for core lifecycle prose.

title: Use case before platform
text: Before choosing a platform, workflow engine, or storage model, the programme should define the service it is trying to operate. The minimum description is the public-health use case, the users of the result, the sampling strategy, the expected output, the turnaround need, and the action that should follow.
reviewerNotes: Foundation fragment for the service-model section.

title: Sampling defines interpretation
text: A genomic result can only answer the question supported by the samples entering the system. Sampling strategy, representativeness, turnaround time, data quality, metadata completeness, and reporting route should therefore be designed together, not treated as separate technical details.
reviewerNotes: General sampling principle only. Organism-specific sampling details remain gaps.

title: Data asset, not one report
text: A routine genomics service should maintain a data asset that can feed more than one product: sample-level reports, outbreak alerts, surveillance summaries, repository submissions, quality review, and later reanalysis. This requires preserved links between sample identifiers, metadata, sequence data, QC outputs, workflow runs, interpreted results, reports, and sharing events.
reviewerNotes: Central fragment for sample-to-decision service framing.

title: Identifier lineage
text: The minimum data model should preserve the lineage from sample or isolate to metadata, sequencing run, raw data, QC result, workflow run, interpreted result, report, sharing event, repository accession, and retention or archive decision. Without that linkage, a technically sound analysis can become hard to interpret, audit, correct, share, or reuse.
reviewerNotes: Reviewed against WHO 2023 data-management text, WHO 2025 platform provenance text, PHE LIMS/report linkage, and Armstrong lab/epi integration.

title: Platform provenance
text: A repository or data-sharing platform should be assessed by more than upload capability. The programme should check metadata scope, provenance, persistent identifiers, access controls, curation, interoperability, documented APIs, reporting functions, and how data contributors and downstream users are acknowledged.
reviewerNotes: Use when expanding repository/platform selection and data-sharing sections.

title: Validate the whole chain
text: Validation should cover the whole service chain, not only the final bioinformatics command. Input material, library preparation, sequencing, QC, workflow execution, interpretation, reporting, user communication, and external quality assessment all affect whether a result can be used for public-health action.
reviewerNotes: Reviewed against PHE validation, WHO data-quality, APHL validation, and clinical microbiology quality-management passages.

title: QC and version provenance
text: Routine results should carry enough provenance to be interpreted later: QC status, QC methods, acceptance thresholds, workflow version, software versions, database versions, reference data, and any exception or override. This is what lets a service explain, repeat, correct, or retire a result when methods change.
reviewerNotes: Use for workflow provenance and change-control sections. A formal release/rollback runbook remains a gap.

title: Governed sharing routes
text: Data sharing should be designed as a set of governed routes, not as a single upload habit. A programme should decide what is shared for operations, national surveillance, public repositories, controlled-access platforms, and later reuse; when each route is used; what metadata travel with the data; who can access it; and what licences, attribution, acknowledgement, withdrawal, or benefit-sharing expectations apply.
reviewerNotes: Reviewed against WHO 2022 principles, WHO 2023 national strategy support tool, and WHO 2025 platform attributes.

title: Storage categories
text: A genomics service should separate active analysis storage, long-term archive, backup copies, retention policy, access control, and recovery planning. Raw data, filtered data, processed data, reports, workflow logs, QC outputs, repository submissions, and dashboards do not all need the same storage class, access rule, backup schedule, or retention period.
reviewerNotes: Reviewed against WHO 2023 storage policy, PHE infrastructure, and PHA4GE data-flow text. Detailed retention periods remain a gap.

title: Recurrent responsibility
text: The operating model should name who owns workflow execution, storage growth, data movement, access management, software updates, infrastructure maintenance, recovery testing, user support, and recurrent cost. Without those owners, a sequencing service can appear technically functional while depending on unbudgeted labour or undocumented local knowledge.
reviewerNotes: Use to connect infrastructure and costing sections.

title: Workforce as service capacity
text: Workforce planning should cover the service, not only the sequencing bench. A routine programme needs laboratory staff, bioinformaticians, data managers, quality leads, IT or security support, reporting scientists, epidemiological interpreters, trainers, and user-support routes. The exact staffing model can vary, but the responsibilities cannot be left unnamed.
reviewerNotes: Staffing ratios remain a gap.

title: Recurrent cost categories
text: A credible budget should count recurrent service categories: people, training, quality management, storage growth, cloud or in-house compute, software licences, maintenance, redundancy, system administration, support, validation, and revalidation. A one-off equipment budget is not a service plan.
reviewerNotes: Numeric cost estimates remain setting-specific gaps.

title: Maturity as risk reduction
text: Use maturity as a way to choose the next service risk to reduce. The useful question is not whether a programme has reached an abstract tier, but which dependency would stop public-health use: one person leaving, internet failing, storage filling, access not being reviewed, a workflow changing without validation, or users not understanding the report.
reviewerNotes: Formal maturity score remains a gap.

title: Reports as decision products
text: A report is a service product, not a file emitted by the pipeline. It should name the user, the public-health question, the timeframe, the method or workflow version, the quality limits, the uncertainty, and the decision it can support.
reviewerNotes: Report wording and uncertainty categories remain a gap.

title: Organism-specific reporting
text: Keep the opening reporting principle common, then vary the practical content by organism and use case. Enteric-bacterial services may report cluster membership, source links, food or animal-health context and AMR. TB services may report transmission hypotheses, outbreak refutation or confirmation, reinfection/reactivation questions and resistance inference. Respiratory-virus services may report variants, lineage dynamics, sampling context, vaccine or risk-assessment relevance and limitations of partial genomes. AMR reporting should state the organism, drug, resistance mechanism, database or rule set, and the limits of genotype-phenotype inference.
reviewerNotes: Specific reporting templates remain unapproved until more pathogen-specific guidance is extracted.

title: Lifecycle as service loop
text: The sample-to-report lifecycle should be run as a service loop, not drawn as a straight line. Routine throughput, failed samples, low-quality runs, delayed reports, corrected results, repository submissions, accession updates, user feedback, and later reanalysis all need a recorded route through the same data asset.
reviewerNotes: Dashboard and field dictionary remain gaps.

title: Exception and service-review register
text: A routine genomics service needs a review register for failed or repeated samples, low-quality runs, missing metadata, ambiguous results, delayed reports, corrected records and recurring service problems. Each row should connect the event to a minimum record, an owner, a decision, a user or report impact, and any corrective action.
reviewerNotes: This is a beta review-register pattern, not an organism-specific QC threshold set or accreditation template.

title: Service model comparison
text: The service model should be named before infrastructure is selected. Routine surveillance, outbreak response, reactive sequencing, prospective surveillance, repository submission, clinical support and research support place different pressure on sampling, turnaround, validation, reporting, storage, access, feedback and staffing.
reviewerNotes: A full table with organism-specific examples remains future work.

title: Data-sharing decision pathway
text: The sharing decision should proceed in order: purpose, data type, minimum metadata, sensitive metadata, quality status, access model, platform or repository, timing, accession or persistent identifier, correction route, withdrawal route, and local owner. This separates operational sharing from national reporting, public repository submission, controlled access, and non-sharing.
reviewerNotes: Jurisdiction-specific legal review remains out of scope.

title: Release and change control
text: A routine service should treat workflow, database, QC-threshold and report changes as controlled releases. Each release needs a reason for change, validation evidence, version record, approval route, communication plan, rollback or emergency-fix criteria, and a trigger for revalidation when the service or public-health interpretation changes.
reviewerNotes: This is beta guidance, not a full accreditation SOP.

title: Beta metadata and accession model
text: For beta, treat metadata as five linked buckets: public minimum metadata, restricted contextual metadata, technical provenance, repository accession fields, and service-management fields. The buckets can be implemented in different systems, but they need a common identifier lineage so a report, repository record, reanalysis or correction can be traced back to the same sample or isolate.
reviewerNotes: This is a beta model, not a full pathogen-specific schema.

title: Beta metadata governance register
text: The beta metadata model should be maintained as an operational register, not only as a field list. The register records identifier lineage, system of record, public or restricted use, correction route, accession or sharing status, review owner, and remaining schema gaps.
reviewerNotes: This is an operational register pattern. It does not define exact repository schemas, controlled vocabularies, legal permissions or completeness KPIs.

title: Beta workforce responsibility template
text: For beta, use a responsibility template rather than a fixed staffing ratio. Every service model should name owners for wet-lab processing, sequencing operation, bioinformatics workflow operation, QC review, validation and change control, data management, storage and backup, access review, reporting, interpretation, user training, incident support, procurement or costing, and programme oversight. The same person may cover more than one function in a small service, but no function should be invisible.
reviewerNotes: Use as a beta role/responsibility map. It does not define FTE, grades, salaries, or staffing ratios.

title: Beta service-review table
text: A beta service review should track routine volume, failed or repeated samples, failed or repeated runs, missing metadata, delayed reports, corrected reports, repository-submission status, user feedback and corrective actions. Each row should connect the event to an owner, decision, user impact and closure status.
reviewerNotes: Renderer currently exposes this as structured prose/technical detail rather than a native table.

title: Beta national capability-domain checklist
text: For national-scale planning, compare capability domains before selecting an implementation model. The beta domains are public-health priorities and users, laboratory network and quality, data and analytics platforms, governance and data sharing, workforce and interpretation, and sustainability and evaluation.
reviewerNotes: This is a beta capability-domain checklist, not a national architecture, governance template or maturity score.

title: Beta implementation dependency matrix
text: Compare implementation models by dependency and fallback, not by a generic ranking. For each local, centralised, cloud, managed-platform, hybrid, collaborative, or external-support model, record who owns the service, where sequencing and analysis happen, who validates workflows, where data are hosted, how access is managed, what internet or power dependency exists, what local capability is built, what happens if the provider or network route fails, and which costs recur.
reviewerNotes: This is a dependency matrix, not a maturity score or procurement recommendation.

title: Beta organism and use-case service-model matrix
text: Use an organism and use-case service-model matrix before prescribing technical details. Rows should include routine surveillance, outbreak response, AMR monitoring, repository submission, clinical or hospital support, metagenomic or mixed-sample use, and research support. Columns should include organism or programme, decision user, sampling route, expected output, turnaround pressure, metadata dependency, QC or validation boundary, reporting route, sharing route, infrastructure dependency, and fallback if the result is delayed or inconclusive.
reviewerNotes: This is a beta matrix to structure thinking. It does not provide organism-specific thresholds, report templates, sampling fractions or metagenomic diagnostic rules.

title: Beta TB, AMR and infection-control reporting fields
text: For TB, AMR and healthcare-associated infection support, keep relatedness, resistance prediction, clinical or infection-control action and uncertainty as separate report fields. The report should state the method or rule set, validation basis, database or workflow version, follow-up testing status, recipient route and what the result cannot prove without epidemiological or clinical context.
reviewerNotes: This is a beta reporting field model. It deliberately avoids pathogen-specific thresholds or treatment advice.

title: Beta enteric and respiratory-virus reporting fields
text: For enteric bacteria and respiratory viruses, reports should make the public-health route visible. Enteric reports should connect sample, cluster, exposure or food/environmental links, AMR or virulence findings, recipients and action route. Respiratory-virus reports should connect sampling frame, collection date and place, genome completeness, lineage or variant call, quality limits, repository or sharing route, and whether further phenotyping or correction is needed.
reviewerNotes: This is a beta field model, not pathogen-specific report wording or thresholds.

title: Beta data-sharing decision table
text: A data-sharing decision should be recorded as a route map rather than a single upload instruction. The beta table separates the public-health purpose, sharing route, data type, metadata, quality status, access model, timing, accession tracking, correction, withdrawal and accountable owner.
reviewerNotes: This table is a beta route map. It must not be read as legal, ethical or repository-specific submission advice.

title: Beta organism metadata field priorities
text: The beta metadata model can separate common lineage fields from organism and use-case priorities. This supports practical design without presenting a repository-specific schema as universal.
reviewerNotes: This is a beta priority table, not a final repository schema or controlled vocabulary.

title: Beta validation evidence package
text: Before a pilot or research workflow becomes a routine public-health service, the beta guide should assemble a validation evidence package covering intended use, comparator or reference material, wet-lab and sequencing acceptance, bioinformatics workflow and reference data, interpretation and reporting boundary, and ongoing monitoring or external comparison.
reviewerNotes: This is a beta evidence-package checklist, not an accredited validation protocol, organism-specific threshold set or local SOP.

title: Beta release and change-control checklist
text: For beta, treat workflow, database, QC-threshold, interpretation-rule and report-template changes as controlled releases. The checklist records what changed, why it changed, what was tested, who approved it, how users are told, and when revalidation is triggered.
reviewerNotes: This is a beta checklist for guide use. It still needs local quality-system and accreditation review before use as an SOP.

title: Beta costing scenario templates
text: Use scenario templates to compare service models before committing to procurement. Each scenario should state the use case, throughput, sequencing model, analysis model, storage model, workforce and support roles, quality-management scope, sharing route, expected benefit, excluded costs and evidence confidence.
reviewerNotes: This is a planning template. It should not be used as a quantitative cost estimate without local data.

title: Procurement and supply-chain resilience
text: A service can fail because purchasing, import, resupply, maintenance or vendor support is slow. The beta guide should make procurement and supply-chain risks visible alongside staffing, storage, compute and quality-management costs.
reviewerNotes: This is a beta resilience checklist. It does not prescribe stock levels, supplier choices, customs procedures or national procurement rules.

title: Beta storage, backup, archive and recovery map
text: For beta, classify genomics data by operational purpose before choosing storage. Active analysis data, raw-read archives, processed outputs, reports, workflow logs, repository records and backups need different access, recovery, retention and ownership decisions.
reviewerNotes: This is a category map. Retention periods, RTO, RPO and deletion rules remain jurisdiction-specific gaps.

title: Beta model-specific responsibility variants
text: The same service functions appear in every implementation model, but the accountable owner changes. For beta, compare responsibility variants for pilot, routine single-pathogen service, national network, managed-platform and hybrid or collaborative models before assigning staffing or procurement.
reviewerNotes: This is a responsibility variant table, not a staffing ratio or procurement recommendation.

title: Beta constraint-response matrix
text: Use wizard constraints as prompts for implementation risk, not as an automatic platform choice. Unreliable internet, limited bioinformatics staffing, weak central IT, missing LIMS, cloud restrictions and external-support dependence should raise specific fallback, ownership, metadata and sustainability questions.
reviewerNotes: This maps beta wizard constraints to planning questions. It is not a validated scoring rule.

title: Beta decision-product map
text: A routine genomics service may produce urgent alerts, sample-level reports, cluster or incident briefings, surveillance summaries, repository or platform updates, and service-review dashboards from the same data asset. Each product should name its user, timing, quality limits, action boundary and feedback route.
reviewerNotes: This is a beta product map. It does not define final report wording, legal sign-off, pathogen-specific thresholds or mandatory dashboard metrics.

title: Beta reporting decision checklist
text: A beta report checklist can name the user, public-health question, timeframe, method, quality limits, uncertainty, action boundary, sharing route and feedback route. This supports consistent decision-use reporting without prescribing pathogen-specific wording.
reviewerNotes: This is a report-component checklist, not final report wording or uncertainty categories.

title: Beta service-review use by implementation stage
text: Use the same service-review rows differently as the programme matures. A pilot should test whether the sample-to-report route is repeatable; a routine service should monitor failures, turnaround, corrections and user impact; a national or network service should review comparability, shared methods, data flow, feedback and support across sites.
reviewerNotes: This is a beta stage-use guide. It still needs review with real pilot, routine and network programmes.

title: Beta SOP handoff checklist
text: Use the release and change-control checklist as a handoff into the local quality system. The beta guide can name what the SOP package should contain, but local laboratory, bioinformatics, quality, accreditation and regulatory owners must approve the actual SOP before it is used for service reporting.
reviewerNotes: This is a beta SOP handoff checklist, not an accredited SOP template or a substitute for local regulatory review.

title: Beta retention and recovery target worksheet
text: Set retention, backup and recovery targets by data category and service use. The source base supports recording criticality, regeneration ability, archive location, access route, backup location, restore testing and deletion authority; it does not support universal retention periods or recovery-time objectives across jurisdictions.
reviewerNotes: This supports local target-setting but deliberately avoids unsupported retention periods, RTOs or RPOs.

title: Beta workforce capacity worksheet
text: Estimate workforce capacity by service function, throughput, turnaround pressure, support model, quality scope and cover requirement. The source base supports costing people, training, quality management, bioinformatics support and user engagement, but it does not yet support universal FTE ratios by pathogen, throughput or implementation model.
reviewerNotes: This is a beta capacity-planning worksheet. It deliberately avoids unsupported FTE ratios.

title: Beta data-sharing governance review
text: Before data leave the programme or move into a shared platform, record the governance review needed for the chosen route. The beta guide can structure questions about public-health purpose, sensitivity, access, terms, attribution, benefit sharing, data residency, correction and withdrawal, but local legal, ethical, privacy and repository-specific requirements still need review.
reviewerNotes: This is a governance-review scaffold, not legal advice or repository-specific submission policy.

title: Beta reporting limitations checklist
text: A beta report should make limitations visible without pretending to have a universal uncertainty taxonomy. At minimum, it should state sample and comparison limits, data quality, method and version, evidence type, interpretation boundary, follow-up needed, and the action the result can and cannot support.
reviewerNotes: This is a limitations checklist. It does not prescribe report wording, legal sign-off, or pathogen-specific uncertainty categories.

title: Beta report-template readiness review
text: Before converting beta reporting fields into prescribed pathogen-specific wording, review whether the required template sources, interpretation rules, QC thresholds, nomenclature rules, repository fields and governance approvals exist for that organism and use case.
reviewerNotes: This is a readiness review for future templates. It should not be read as current pathogen-specific report wording.

title: Organism-specific interpretation guardrails
text: Use organism-specific guardrails to separate what a genomic signal can support from what it cannot prove alone. Enteric, respiratory-virus, TB/AMR and healthcare-associated-infection reports all need sampling context, metadata, method/version records, uncertainty, and a defined action boundary before results are used for public-health decisions.
reviewerNotes: This is an interpretation guardrail table. It does not prescribe thresholds, clinical action rules or final report wording.

title: Beta shared workflow adoption checklist
text: Shared workflows can reduce local development effort, but a programme still needs to record the workflow source, maintainer, version, execution environment, inputs, outputs, validation boundary, reportable changes, approval route, rollback plan and reanalysis impact before using the workflow for public-health reporting.
reviewerNotes: This is an adoption checklist for shared workflows. It is not an endorsement of any specific workflow ecosystem.

title: Beta organism profile QA checks
text: Use profile checks to confirm that the dynamic guide brings the relevant organism and use-case sections forward for enteric bacteria, respiratory viruses, TB/AMR and healthcare-associated infection. These checks support beta tailoring, but they do not replace review by disease-programme users.
reviewerNotes: This is a beta profile QA scaffold. Disease-programme user review remains required.

title: Beta maturity risk-reduction worksheet
text: Use maturity as a structured planning conversation that identifies the next service risk to reduce across use-case ownership, sample and metadata lineage, workflow validation and provenance, storage and access recovery, workforce cover, recurrent cost, dependencies and improvement review. Do not treat the beta worksheet as a validated maturity score.
reviewerNotes: This is a beta planning worksheet, not a validated maturity rubric or scoring instrument.

title: Sample-to-decision route map
text: A routine genomics service should map each sample-to-decision step to the record that must persist, the decision or product that record supports, and the exception route that should be owned. The route map should cover sample entry, metadata linkage, sequencing and QC, workflow execution, interpretation and reporting, sharing, storage, review and correction.
reviewerNotes: This is a beta route map, not a complete organism-specific field dictionary or SOP.
